key_principles: Cirrhotic background significantly increases suspicion for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).
Tumor nodules often appear tan or grey-white, firm, sometimes showing central yellow necrosis.
HCC may show an indistinct border or a thin grey pseudocapsule.
Vascular invasion may appear as dark-red thrombus-like structures within vessel lumens.
decision_logic: If cirrhosis + tan nodule + irregular border → malignant, favor HCC.
If no cirrhosis + solitary well-circumscribed nodule → consider hepatocellular adenoma first.
A whitish tumor abutting major bile ducts favors intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma.

low_power: Loss of normal hepatic lobular architecture with replacement by irregular sheets or cords.
Presence of pseudolobules indicates cirrhosis in background liver.
Search for boundaries between tumor and non-tumor areas to identify infiltration.
medium_power: Classical HCC shows trabeculae 2–3 cells thick with sinusoidal spacing.
Pseudoglandular patterns may contain bile plugs.
Solid patterns correlate with lower differentiation.
Evidence of malignancy includes capsule invasion and vascular invasion.
high_power: Nuclei enlarged to 1.5–2× normal size with irregular contours.
Prominent eosinophilic nucleoli with coarse, uneven chromatin.
Frequent mitotic figures including abnormal forms.
Cytoplasm often eosinophilic or clear due to glycogen/fat.

HCC_markers: Glypican-3 – supports malignancy in hepatocellular tumors.
CD34 – diffuse sinusoidal capillarization characteristic of HCC.

HCC_vs_Dysplastic_Nodule: HCC: GPC3+, Hsp70+, GS+ (any two positive strongly indicate HCC).
Dysplastic nodule: typically negative for all three markers.
ClearCell_HCC_vs_RCC: Renal cell carcinoma phenotype: HepPar-1−, CA-IX+, CD10+.
